entry.title: AI Comes to the Classroom: Updated Academic Lesson on SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
A Lesson That Keeps Pace with the Technology

If you have used the Academic Lesson on "Designing Stories with SAP Analytics Cloud" before, you already know it as a solid bridge between textbook concepts and real-world analytics tooling. The lesson walks students through data exploration in SAP Analytics Cloud, then into the design of impactful data stories, all with free access to SAP Learning Hub, student edition courses and practice systems.

The latest release, **24-sac-001d**, builds on that foundation with a meaningful addition: Activity 3: Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud.

What Is New in This Update
A Third Activity Focused on AI and Augmented Analytics

The previous version (24-sac-002c, August 2025) included two activities:

Activity 1 - Exploring SAP Analytics Cloud (navigation, modeling, planning features)
Activity 2 - Designing Stories in SAP Analytics Cloud (building and presenting data-driven Stories)

This update adds:

Activity 3 - Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud

Activity 3 draws from the SAP Learning Hub, student edition course of the same name and introduces students to two units of content:

1. Introducing AI, Machine Learning, and Augmented Analytics - foundational concepts on how AI and machine learning underpin modern business intelligence.
2. Using SAP Analytics Cloud Smart Features - hands-on work with Just Ask, the natural-language query feature that lets users type a question and receive an auto-generated visualization, and Smart Insights, which surfaces data patterns automatically.

Students complete the two units (approximately 52 minutes of course content), earn passing scores on the associated quizzes, and work through a practice exercise where they build a Just Ask Canvas Story live in SAP Analytics Cloud.

New Discussion Questions That Go Beyond the Tool

Activity 3 also comes with three discussion questions designed to engage student critical thinking:

  1. Responsible use of AI-generated visualizations: Students consider what happens when a manager asks them to drop a Just Ask chart into a quarterly business review. Who is responsible for accuracy? What should be verified before presenting AI-generated output?
  2. Data quality and forecasting: Students examine how flawed input data affects forecast reliability, connecting back to the "Garbage In, Garbage Out" principle from Activity 2.
  3. Audience-driven design with AI content: Students compare how a 12-month sales forecast story should be designed differently for an internal sales team versus executive leadership, including how to handle confidence intervals and the visual distinction between actuals and predictions.

These questions are ready to use as class discussions, group activities, essay prompts, or inter-teaching exercises. Suggested answers are included for instructors as a starting point of discussion.

Why This Update Matters for Your Classroom

The shift from specialist-driven analytics to business-user-driven analytics is one of the most significant trends in the data landscape. Organizations increasingly expect graduates, regardless of discipline, to be comfortable interpreting data and questioning AI-generated outputs. 

Here is what makes it practical for teaching

Low barrier to entry - Activity 3 requires only about 52 minutes of course content and one practice exercise. You can add it to an existing assignment without overhauling your syllabus.

No prerequisites beyond the lesson itself -  Students who complete Activities 1 and 2 have all the context they need. The lesson remains accessible to undergraduate and graduate students across business, information systems, social sciences, natural sciences, engineering, medicine, journalism, and beyond.

Free access - The SAP Learning Hub student edition, the courses, and the practice systems are all available at no cost to students and lecturers at institutions of higher education.

Real AI, not simulated -  Students interact with actual AI-powered features in SAP Analytics Cloud. They type natural-language questions, receive generated charts, and evaluate the results, it is hands-on experience with enterprise-grade augmented analytics.

Critical thinking built in - The discussion questions encourage students to treat AI as a starting point, not a finished product. That mindset, evaluating, refining, and contextualizing AI output is one of the most important skill students can develop right now.

How to Get Started

The updated lesson is available for download in two formats:

PDF Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446
Word Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525

The Word version is fully editable, so you can adapt the student instructions, deliverables, and discussion questions to fit your course design. Add requirements, remove what does not apply, and make it your own.

To access the courses and practice systems, students and lecturers will need an SAP Universal ID and enrollment in the SAP Learning Hub, student edition. If this is your first time using materials as part of the SAP University Alliances program, visit the Getting Started resources linked within the lesson document for setup guidance.

A Note on Flexibility

One of the strengths of this Academic Lesson has always been its adaptability. You do not have to assign all three Activities. You can assign any combination that fits your learning outcomes. You can use the discussion questions as graded essays, informal class discussions, or skip them entirely.

That said, if you are looking for one update to bring into your next offering, Activity 3 is a strong candidate. Giving students hands-on time with AI-augmented analytics, alongside the critical thinking prompts to evaluate what the AI produces, is a combination that serves them well regardless of where their careers take them.

entry.title: SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights
entry.content:
1. Why CPG Organizations Struggle with Fragmented Data and Slow Insights 

Key account managers (KAMs), IT admins, and partners in the Consumer Products industry all wrestle with the same trio of issues: fragmented data across ERP, CRM, and market sources; long and fragile integrations for each new initiative; and analytics that take too long to trust, let alone act on.

The result is slower promotion cycles, delayed deductions and trade claims reconciliation, and AI projects that stall because the “single source of truth” never quite materializes. SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) exists to break that cycle—and when SAP Revenue Growth Management (RGM) integrates as part of the Consumer Products Intelligence solution on SAP BDC, the platform turns integration projects into a guided setup and makes governed, AI‑ready data the default state.

 

2. Key Concepts Explained: SAP BDC, Data Products & Intelligent Apps 

SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) is a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data—enabling data management, analytics, and AI capabilities. It brings together the existing capabilities of SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse (BW) and incorporates SAP Databricks, line of business data products, and SAP’s open data ecosystem partners to create an integrated solution. 

Data Products (DPs) are curated, reusable datasets—packaged with metadata and APIs—so applications can consume or produce data without point‑to‑point integration. In this model, a producer is any application that creates and publishes data products into SAP BDC, while a consumer is any application or tool that reads those data products from SAP BDC to support analytics, AI, or operational processes. 

SAP Business Data Cloud Intelligent Applications are SAP‑managed apps built on certified data products that deliver embedded analytics and AI‑powered experiences out of the box.  

 

3. How it All Connects: Architecture Overview 

 

The diagram above illustrates the Consumer Products Intelligence solution in SAP Business Data Cloud—which includes SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

A CPG manufacturer uses SAP Revenue Growth Management to manage trade promotions. When a KAM creates a promotion within the application, SAP Revenue Growth Management consumes S/4HANA master data and sales- and finance-related data products via SAP BDC to power its core features, including promotion planning and embedded analytics. 

Once the promotion is approved and released, SAP Revenue Growth Management writes promotion data back to S/4HANA—supporting processes such as Claims and Condition Contract Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

At the same time, SAP Revenue Growth Management produces its own data products back into SAP BDC, making them available to SAP Analytics Cloud. These dashboards help KAMs quickly view spend vs. uplift and spot which accounts in their territory delivered the strongest promotion ROI—so those winning promotions can be copied to drive future revenue growth. 

 

4. How SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC Delivers Value 

4.1. Automated and Seamless Integration Across SAP Systems 

Instead of wiring SAP Revenue Growth Management to each source system, users add the solution to an SAP BDC “formation” (a logical grouping of your SAP systems) in SAP for Me. The platform then handles the essential integration steps and installs the required data products for users—reducing manual setup and ambiguity. For example, SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically receives the S/4HANA data products it needs without manual setup.  

 

4.2. Harmonized SAP Data: Powered by Data Products and One Domain Model (ODM) 

SAP Revenue Growth Management's embedded analytics feature—five dashboards covering promotion performance, revenue, spend vs. uplift, volume, and profit and loss—has always been built into the application. With the SAP BDC integration, the underlying data powering these dashboards now flows through standardized, governed data products rather than point-to-point connections. Because all SAP data is exposed as standardized data products following SAP's One Domain Model (ODM), data across different SAP systems is harmonized by design—making it effortless to combine and analyze across systems. This lays the foundation for increasingly powerful analytics capabilities going forward, including customizable dashboards and broader cross‑system analysis. 

  

4.3. Extensibility: Unlocking Third-Party Data for a More Complete Picture 

The integration of SAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP BDC also opens the door for customers to enrich their analysis with data from outside the SAP ecosystem. As SAP BDC continues to evolve, customers will be able to import their own third-party data into SAP BDC in a standardized way, enabling it to flow into SAP Revenue Growth Management without custom pipelines or one-off integrations. 

For example, a beverage company will be able to import sell-out data—such as point-of-sale records from retailers showing actual consumer purchases—and combine it with promotion and account plan data directly within SAP Revenue Growth Management for scenario-based analysis. The result: a more complete picture of promotion effectiveness, grounded in both SAP data and real-world market signals. 

 

5. What’s New: SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC 

5.1. Streamlined Data Product Consumption Through End-to-End Provisioning Automation 

Guided Wizard in SAP for Me

Previously, provisioning required separate manual steps—creating a formation, configuring connectivity, and installing data products individually. Now, as an intelligent application, the Consumer Products Intelligence solution in SAP Business Data Cloud provides a guided wizard in SAP for Me that handles tenant creation, SAP BDC integration setup, and connectivity configuration—without IT admins needing to interpret long setup guides. 

 

Automatic Dependency Management

SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically consumes the SAP‑managed data products it depends on. IT admins and onboarding partners no longer need to do any manual work—all relevant dependencies are automatically identified, installed, and activated during the provisioning process. 

 

5.2. Standardized Data Product Production for Richer Insights 

Previously, accessing application data required custom API integrations and one-time development effort for each connection—costly to build and difficult to maintain. Now, as SAP Revenue Growth Management processes key business data such as promotion plans, baselines, actuals, and analytics, it produces its own standardized data products into SAP BDC. These data products share a harmonized data model with other SAP data products, enabling customers to conduct holistic analysis across their data landscape—and empowering agentic AI capabilities in Joule to deliver insightful suggestions and actions grounded in rich, connected business data. 

 

6. Join Our Co-Innovation Initiative 

The integration ofSAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP Business Data Cloud marks a significant step toward faster setup, governed analytics, and connected insights for Consumer Products companies. 

By unifying inbound consumption of S/4HANA data products and outbound data products from SAP Revenue Growth Management for analytics and extension, we aim to help CPG companies stay agile and competitive in a rapidly changing market. 

If you’re an existing or prospective customer willing to explore these capabilities firsthand, you're welcome to join our co‑innovation program. Contact us at RGM_Engage@sap.com to start the journey toward modernizing trade promotion execution and unlocking governed insights across commercial processes. 

 

More Information

Product Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management 

Documentation on SAP Help Portal

Topic Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management

    • Introducing Calendar View in SAP Revenue Growth Management: A Visual Leap for Strategic Planning
    • Decoding Promotional Success: Embedded Analytics in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Unlocking Trade Spend Control Efficiency with Funds Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Consolidating Customer Planning: Unleash Growth with SAP Revenue Growth Management’s Account Plan
    • Elevate Your Promotion Strategy: Baseline Management with SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • Driving Promotion Excellence: Streamlined Creation and Copying with SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Financial Execution & Claims for Trade Promotions with SAP Revenue Growth Management & SAP Cloud ERP
    • Master Your KPIs: How SAP Revenue Growth Management Boosts Data-Driven Growth 
    • Modernize Planning: Universal Hierarchy in SAP Revenue Growth Management for SAP Cloud ERP Customers
    • Promotion Creation Reimagined: AI‑Assisted Single & Bulk Workflows in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • One Plan, Multiple Measures: Driving Growth with Flexible Units in SAP Revenue Growth Management

entry.title: Bringing Analytics to Life with Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst
entry.content:
For more than 15 years, Université de Sherbrooke has been a member of the SAP University Alliances program, and our School of Management has continuously benefited from this global academic community to innovate in the teaching of enterprise systems. Over the years, SAP technologies have allowed us to move beyond theory and immerse students in realistic, hands-on learning experiences. With the launch of Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, this pedagogical journey has taken an exciting new turn, one that immediately resonated with our students.

From the classroom to the rink: a natural fit

When we first introduced Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst in class, one of the most striking aspects was how easy it was to deploy. As a free, cloud-based solution built on SAP Analytics Cloud, the game required no complex setup, no local installations, and no steep learning curve. Within a single class session, students were able to log in, explore dashboards, and begin analyzing data. This simplicity made it easy to integrate the game seamlessly into existing courses. 

The hockey context immediately grabbed attention. Even students who were not die-hard fans quickly became engaged, as the scenario provided an intuitive way to understand abstract analytics concepts. The connection between data, performance metrics, and decision-making became tangible almost instantly.

Student reactions: curiosity turns into engagement

The reaction from students was overwhelmingly positive. What began as curiosity (“We’re analyzing NHL data in class?”) quickly turned into deep engagement. The competitive and playful nature of the game encouraged participation, discussion, and collaboration. Perhaps most importantly, the game lowered the intimidation factor often associated with analytics. Students who initially felt less confident with data became more comfortable experimenting, exploring visualizations, and asking questions. 

What students actually learned

Beyond engagement, the learning outcomes were clear. Students developed core analytics competencies, including data interpretation, KPI analysis, and storytelling with dashboards. Because everything is grounded in SAP Analytics Cloud, students also gained hands-on experience with a professional-grade analytics platform used by thousands of organizations worldwide. This connection between academic learning and real business tools significantly enhanced the perceived relevance of the activity.

The availability of multiple versions (novice and advanced), with different gameplay scenarios (goal scoring, goalies) also allowed us to adapt the experience to different levels, from introductory analytics courses to more advanced classes.

Advice for fellow educators

For educators considering Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, my main advice is simple: start small and let the game do the work. You don’t need to redesign an entire course to benefit from it. Even a short in-class activity can spark curiosity and meaningful discussions. Encourage students to explore, make mistakes, and reflect on their decisions rather than focusing solely on “winning” the game.

Finally, embrace the storytelling aspect. The real power of this approach lies in how it connects data, technology, and human decision-making within a narrative students care about.

A powerful addition to analytics education

Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst represents a compelling example of how partnerships between academia, industry, and sports organizations (SAP, the NHL, and the ERPsim Lab at HEC Montréal) can produce innovative, engaging, and pedagogically sound learning experiences. For us at Université de Sherbrooke, it has become a valuable tool to enrich analytics education, spark student motivation, and bridge the gap between classroom learning and real-world practice.

-Prof. Olivier Caya, Université de Sherbrooke (Canada)
